1: Listen to me, you who pursue righteousness — you who seek YHWH. Look to the rock from which you were hewn and to the quarry from which you were dug. The double imperative listen / look summons the discouraged remnant. Rock and quarry are the ancestral origin — the geological stability of Abraham's family as template for restoration from exile's rubble.
2: Look to Abraham your father and to Sarah who bore you — for he was but one when I called him, that I might bless him and multiply him. The one-to-many logic: Abraham was single when called; the covenant produced a nation. The exilic remnant, however small, stands in the same structural position as the one ancestor. Numerical weakness does not disqualify divine promise.
3: For YHWH will comfort Zion — he will comfort all her waste places. He will make her wilderness like Eden and her desert like the garden of YHWH — joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the voice of song. The wilderness-to-Eden reversal is the creation-restoration vision. Eden as the template for end-state restoration appears in Ezekiel's river vision (Ezek 47) and the Revelation's new Jerusalem. The wilderness between Babylon and Jerusalem becomes a garden.
4: Pay attention to me, my people — give ear to me, my nation. A law will go out from me and I will set my justice as a light to the peoples. The Torah-light going to the nations extends the Servant's light-for-goyim commission (Isaiah 49:6) to YHWH himself. ANE treaty law was the great king's gift to vassal peoples; here YHWH's cosmic instruction extends beyond Israel to all nations.
5: My righteousness is near — my salvation has gone out and my arms will judge the peoples. The coastlands hope in me and for my arm they wait. The divine warrior extends justice to the coastlands — the furthest reaches of the known world. Coastlands (iyim) recurs through Second Isaiah as the representative extremity of gentile geography.
6: Lift up your eyes to the heavens and look at the earth beneath — for the heavens vanish like smoke, the earth wears out like a garment, and its inhabitants die like gnats. But my salvation will be forever and my righteousness will never be dismayed. Cosmic transience contrasts with eternal salvation: the most permanent-seeming things (sky, earth) are temporary; YHWH's covenant fidelity alone is permanent. This grounds trust in the most enduring possible foundation.
7: Listen to me, you who know righteousness — the people in whose heart is my law. Fear not the reproach of man and be not dismayed at their revilings. The community addressed has internalized Torah (law in the heart anticipates Jeremiah 31:33). Their enemy is not physical assault but social reproach — the ANE honor-shame pressure of ridicule and contempt from the surrounding culture.
8: For the moth will eat them like a garment and the worm will eat them like wool — but my righteousness will be forever and my salvation to all generations. The moth-and-worm decay image (also Isaiah 50:9) frames the oppressors as perishable fabric. The covenant community endures beyond the empires that mock them.
9: Awake, awake, put on strength, O arm of YHWH — awake as in days of old, the generations of long ago. Was it not you who cut Rahab in pieces and pierced the dragon? The double imperative awake, awake summons the divine warrior. Rahab here is not the Jericho innkeeper but the chaos-sea-monster of ANE cosmogony — cognate with Babylonian Tiamat, Ugaritic Lotan. YHWH's Exodus defeat of Egypt is reread through the ANE chaos-combat myth lens: he defeated both the historical empire and the cosmic dragon behind it.
10: Was it not you who dried up the sea, the waters of the great deep — who made the depths of the sea a way for the redeemed to pass over? The Exodus sea-parting is the central historical proof of YHWH's power over the chaos waters. The same power is now invoked for the new Exodus from Babylon — the redeemed will again cross what seemed impassable.
11: And the ransomed of YHWH shall return and come to Zion with singing — everlasting joy shall be upon their heads. They shall obtain gladness and joy, and sorrow and sighing shall flee away. This verse is nearly identical to Isaiah 35:10 — a deliberate intertextual echo that frames the entire intervening section (Isaiah 36–51) as the theological bridge between the two identical conclusions. The ransomed are the go'el-redeemed community.
12: I, I myself, am he who comforts you — who are you that you fear man who dies? The emphatic double first-person (anochi anochi) is the divine self-assertion formula. The rhetorical question inverts the honor-shame calculus: fearing a mortal human rather than the immortal creator is an epistemological failure. The human oppressor is categorically finite; YHWH is not.
13: You have forgotten YHWH your maker who stretched out the heavens and laid the foundations of the earth — and you feared continually because of the wrath of the oppressor when he sets himself to destroy. Creator-amnesia generates tyrant-fear: forgetting who made heaven and earth leaves the oppressor seeming like the largest power in the field of vision.
14: He who is bowed down shall quickly be released — he shall not die and go down to the pit. His food shall not lack. The prisoner-release announcement is concrete: the exiled community bowed under Babylonian servitude will be released before death (not post-mortem) and will not starve in the pit. Pit (shachat) is the underworld destination of the dishonored dead.
15: For I am YHWH your God who stirs up the sea so that its waves roar — YHWH of hosts is his name. The storm-sea imagery is both creation and covenant: YHWH controls the chaos waters. YHWH of hosts (YHWH tsvaot) is the divine warrior title — commander of the heavenly armies. Military and cosmic sovereignty converge.
16: I have put my words in your mouth and covered you in the shadow of my hand — establishing the heavens and laying the foundation of the earth and saying to Zion, You are my people. Words-in-mouth is the formal prophetic commissioning formula (Jeremiah 1:9; Deuteronomy 18:18). YHWH's creative word that made the cosmos is the same word now placed in the Servant/prophet's mouth for the new creation.
17: Rouse yourself, rouse yourself, stand up, O Jerusalem — you who have drunk from the hand of YHWH the cup of his wrath. You have drunk to the dregs the bowl, the cup of staggering, and drained it. The cup-of-wrath metaphor is standard in ANE treaty-curse literature: the vassal who breaks covenant drinks the cup of divine judgment. Jerusalem has already drained it completely — the punishment is finished.
18: There is none to guide her among all the sons she has borne — there is none to take her by the hand among all the sons she has brought up. Leaderlessness is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy — the community lacks competent leadership as sign of divine withdrawal. Jerusalem is a mother abandoned by all her children, unable to navigate without a guide.
19: These two things have come upon you — who will console you? Devastation and destruction, famine and sword — who will comfort you? The four-fold disaster catalog (devastation, destruction, famine, sword) covers both the military siege and its aftermath. The rhetorical question invites YHWH's answer, which comes in Isaiah 51:22: I am the one who contends for you.
20: Your sons have fainted — they lie at the head of every street like an antelope in a net. They are full of the wrath of YHWH, the rebuke of your God. The antelope-in-net image captures complete helplessness: a creature too exhausted or entrapped to move. The streets filled with collapsed bodies evokes the aftermath of the Babylonian siege of 586 BCE.
21: Therefore hear this, you who are afflicted — you who are drunk, but not with wine. The formula drunk but not with wine distinguishes divine-wrath intoxication from ordinary inebriation. The covenant-curse literature used drunkenness as metaphor for the disorientation of judgment; YHWH's people have experienced this sober horror.
22: Thus says your Lord, YHWH, your God who pleads the cause of his people: Behold, I have taken from your hand the cup of staggering — the bowl of my wrath you shall drink no more. The divine reversal: the cup transferred from Israel's hand. YHWH as legal advocate (rib) intercedes for the people he had judged. The punishment is definitively over.
23: And I will put it into the hand of your tormentors who said to you, Bow down that we may pass over — and you made your back like the ground and like the street for them to pass over. The specific humiliation — human backs used as pavement — appears in ANE military victory reliefs and inscriptions depicting conquered peoples as literal footstools. The cup of wrath passes from Jerusalem to Babylon.
